text: I am the man who accompanied Jacqueline Kennedy to Paris, and I have enjoyed it.
speaker: John F. Kennedy
context: Self-deprecating opening line at a press luncheon at the Palais de Chaillot in Paris on June 2, 1961, during a state visit to France.

source.title: Remarks and Question and Answer Period at the Press Luncheon in Paris | The American Presidency Project

text: I will not make age an issue of this campaign. I am not going to exploit, for political purposes, my opponent's youth and inexperience.
speaker: Ronald Reagan
context: His famous comeback at the 1984 debate against Walter Mondale

source.title: CPD: October 21, 1984 Debate Transcript | Commission on Presidential Debates

text: I am a Ford, not a Lincoln. My addresses will never be as eloquent as Mr. Lincoln's. But I will do my very best to equal his brevity and his plain speaking.
speaker: Gerald Ford
context: Self-deprecating remark Ford made on December 6, 1973, immediately after taking the oath of office as the 40th Vice President (following Spiro Agnew's resignation).

text: I do not like broccoli. And I haven't liked it since I was a little kid, and my mother made me eat it. And I'm President of the United States, and I'm not going to eat any more broccoli.
speaker: George H. W. Bush
context: Bush's lighthearted declaration at his March 22, 1990 news conference, when asked whether he had 'lost the broccoli vote' after it emerged he'd banned broccoli from Air Force One.

source.title: George H.W. Bush: Hater of Broccoli - Smart Politics (University of Minnesota Libraries)

text: The last time I was this high, I was trying to decide on my major.
speaker: Barack Obama
context: Self-deprecating joke at his final White House Correspondents' Dinner on April 30, 2016, riffing on how his approval ratings kept climbing in his last year in office ('in my final year, my approval ratings keep going up').

source.title: Remarks by the President at the White House Correspondents' Dinner | whitehouse.gov (Obama White House Archives)

text: The Internet is not something that you just dump something on. It's not a big truck. It's a series of tubes.
speaker: Ted Stevens
context: Republican U.S. Senator from Alaska, then chairman of the Senate Commerce Committee, attempting to describe how the internet works during a June 28, 2006 committee debate on net neutrality.

text: An old friend of mine once told me, you know, just be yourself - unless you suck.
speaker: John Kennedy
context: Republican U.S. Senator from Louisiana, known for his folksy one-liners, sharing a piece of self-deprecating advice. He used the line in a Jan. 25, 2018 Fox News interview. Innocuous and funny, with no target or policy edge.

source.title: 15 times Sen. John Kennedy got folksy, snarky or both | nola.com

text: I've now been in 57 states? I think one left to go.
speaker: Barack Obama
context: Then a Democratic U.S. Senator from Illinois campaigning for president, Obama misspoke at a May 9, 2008 event in Beaverton, Oregon, after a long stretch of travel (he meant 47).

text: I will do it myself where does he live?
speaker: Cory Booker
context: Cory Booker, then the Democratic Mayor of Newark, NJ, replied on Twitter when a resident said her elderly father needed his driveway shoveled during a major snowstorm. He showed up with volunteers within minutes.

source.title: Cory Booker Shovels Snow After Twitter Request (VIDEO) | HuffPost

text: They misunderestimated me.
speaker: George W. Bush
context: George W. Bush, then the sitting Republican Governor of Texas and a presidential candidate, coined this signature malapropism on Nov. 6, 2000 in Bentonville, Arkansas. A classic, self-aimed, innocuous Bushism with no target or controversy.

text: I think this is the most extraordinary collection of talent, of human knowledge, that has ever been gathered together at the White House, with the possible exception of when Thomas Jefferson dined alone.
speaker: John F. Kennedy
context: Opening of President Kennedy's toast at a dinner honoring Western Hemisphere Nobel Prize winners, April 29, 1962 — a famously gracious, witty compliment to the assembled laureates by invoking Jefferson's solitary genius.

source.title: Remarks at a Dinner Honoring Nobel Prize Winners of the Western Hemisphere | The American Presidency Project

text: I am extraordinarily patient, provided I get my own way in the end.
speaker: Margaret Thatcher
context: Self-aware, tongue-in-cheek remark by the British Prime Minister in an interview published in The Observer, 4 April 1989 (as catalogued by the Margaret Thatcher Centre's quotes page). Speaker was a UK Conservative Prime Minister.

text: Where my reason, imagination or interest were not engaged, I would not or I could not learn.
speaker: Winston Churchill
context: From Churchill's own autobiography 'My Early Life: A Roving Commission' (1930), in the chapter recalling his schooldays at Harrow where he was placed in the lowest form.

source.title: My Early Life, by Winston S. Churchill (Project Gutenberg Canada full text)

text: He said he felt like a little boy who had stubbed his toe in the dark. That he was too old to cry, but it hurt too much to laugh.
speaker: Adlai Stevenson
context: Quoting a Lincoln anecdote to describe how he felt conceding the 1952 presidential election, Springfield, Illinois, Nov. 5, 1952.

source.title: The American Presidency Project: Remarks Conceding the Presidential Election in Springfield, Illinois

text: Few thought he was even a starter / There were many who thought themselves smarter / But he ended PM / CH and OM / An earl and a knight of the garter.
speaker: Clement Attlee
context: A self-mocking limerick the former British PM wrote about himself in a letter to his brother Tom Attlee, April 8, 1956.

text: In 2008 my slogan was, "Yes We Can." In 2013 my slogan was, "Control-Alt-Delete."
speaker: Barack Obama
context: Joking about the rocky HealthCare.gov rollout at the 2014 White House Correspondents' Dinner

source.title: The Obama White House Archives — Remarks by the President at the White House Correspondents' Dinner (2014)

text: I am Al Gore, I used to be the next president of the United States
speaker: Al Gore
context: His self-deprecating opening line at a Harvard Kennedy School lecture, October 4, 2001, after losing the 2000 election

source.title: Harvard Gazette — Gore calls for unity

text: My favorite was The Scrunchie Chronicles: 112 Countries and It's Still All about My Hair
speaker: Hillary Clinton
context: Joking about a rejected memoir title, poking fun at coverage of her hair, in 2014

source.title: The Week — Hillary Clinton jokes she considered calling her memoir The Scrunchie Chronicles

text: I admit it -- last year was rough.
speaker: Barack Obama
context: At the 2014 White House Correspondents' Dinner, Obama opened his self-deprecating riff by candidly conceding his difficult 2013.

source.title: Remarks by the President at White House Correspondents' Dinner (May 3, 2014) | whitehouse.gov archives

text: Worst damnfool mistake I ever made was letting myself be elected Vice President of the United States. Should have stuck with my old chores as Speaker of the House. I gave up the second most important job in the Government for one that didn't amount to a hill of beans. I spent eight long years as Mr. Roosevelt's spare tire.
speaker: John Nance Garner
context: FDR's first vice president ("Cactus Jack"), ruefully mocking his own decision to take the job and his powerlessness in it, after leaving office.

text: I had hoped to be back here this week under different circumstances, running for re-election. But you know the old saying: you win some, you lose some. And then there's that little-known third category.
speaker: Al Gore
context: Opening his 2004 Democratic National Convention address with a self-deprecating joke about his contested 2000 presidential loss.

text: I have now come to the conclusion never again to think of marrying, and for this reason; I can never be satisfied with anyone who would be blockhead enough to have me.
speaker: Abraham Lincoln
context: A candidly self-mocking line from a young Lincoln's letter, poking fun at his own eligibility as a suitor.

text: I thought I looked real cute in my uniform, and then I heard a boy yell from the bleachers, 'Make that basket, bird legs.' And my greatest fear is that same guy is somewhere out there in the audience tonight, and he's going to cut me down to size.
speaker: Ann Richards
context: The Texas governor opening her celebrated 1988 Democratic National Convention keynote with a self-deprecating story about her eighth-grade basketball days.

source.title: Ann Richards, DNC keynote address, 1988 - Speakola

text: I was criticized for being too much concerned with the average Canadians. I can't help that; I am one of them!
speaker: John Diefenbaker
context: Canada's 13th prime minister, disarmingly turning a criticism into a humble admission about his own ordinariness.

text: How can you govern a country that has two hundred and forty-six varieties of cheese?
speaker: Charles de Gaulle
context: The French president and WWII Free France leader, expressing wry exasperation at the ungovernability of France; recorded in Ernest Mignon's 1962 collection Les Mots du General.

text: It's a good thing to be laughed at. It's better than to be ignored.
speaker: Harold Macmillan
context: The British Prime Minister, in a handwritten note to the Postmaster General who wanted to take action against the satirical TV program 'That Was The Week That Was' - a disarmingly self-deprecating shrug at being mocked.

text: It's hard to lead a cavalry charge if you think you look funny on a horse.
speaker: Adlai Stevenson II
context: The two-time Democratic presidential nominee and UN ambassador, known for his wit; a characteristically self-deprecating line about self-confidence and self-image.

text: Freedom is the right to be wrong, not the right to do wrong.
speaker: John Diefenbaker
context: The Canadian Prime Minister, in a candid, aphoristic reflection on liberty (11 March 1958).

text: Nothing so dates a man as to decry the younger generation.
speaker: Adlai Stevenson II
context: The Democratic statesman, in a speech at the University of Wisconsin, Madison (8 October 1952) - a dry, self-aware needle at those who complain about the young.

text: The disappointment of manhood succeeds to the delusion of youth: let us hope that the heritage of old age is not despair.
speaker: Benjamin Disraeli
context: The British Prime Minister and novelist, in a candidly reflective aside on the disappointments of the ages of life (from his fiction, Book VIII, Chapter 4).

text: It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant.
speaker: Zohran Mamdani
context: NYC Mayor Mamdani backing off his promise to arrest Netanyahu, Tuesday statement

source.title: Politics — Mamdani says he doesn’t have legal authority to arrest Netanyahu
